nodes.label: Skills & Employability
nodes.description: Human capital in the form of skills and competencies that determine market employability

nodes.label: Income Generation
nodes.description: The process of earning money through labor or services (salary or freelance)

nodes.label: Cash Inflow
nodes.description: Positive monetary flow entering an individual's financial system as a result of income

nodes.label: Capital Allocation Decision
nodes.description: The deliberate choice of how to distribute available cash across competing financial uses

nodes.label: Consumption (Expenses)
nodes.description: Spending on goods and services that sustains current lifestyle but depletes cash

nodes.label: Savings (Buffer)
nodes.description: Liquid cash reserve held as a financial safety buffer against volatility or emergencies

nodes.label: Investment (Growth)
nodes.description: Deployment of capital into assets expected to appreciate or generate future returns

nodes.label: Lifestyle State
nodes.description: The quality and standard of living maintained through ongoing consumption expenditure

nodes.label: Cash Reserve
nodes.description: Accumulated liquid savings providing financial security and resilience

nodes.label: Asset Base
nodes.description: Portfolio of owned assets built through investment that can generate future income streams

nodes.label: Future Income Capacity
nodes.description: Enhanced ability to generate income in future periods, driven by security and asset returns

nodes.label: Lifestyle-Skills Feedback
nodes.description: personal finance domain → human capital domain bridge: consumption quality reinforces skill development capacity

edges.evidence: Human capital is a precondition for participating in labor markets and generating earnings
Labor or freelance activity directly produces monetary inflows
Available cash necessitates a decision on how to deploy it across uses
A portion of allocated capital is directed toward current consumption
A portion of allocated capital is retained as liquid savings buffer
A portion of allocated capital is deployed into growth-oriented assets
Ongoing expenditure on goods/services sustains and defines lifestyle quality
Consistent saving behavior accumulates into a measurable cash reserve
Invested capital compounds into an asset portfolio over time
Financial security buffer reduces risk and enables pursuit of higher-yield opportunities
Assets generate returns, dividends, or passive income streams that augment future earnings
Compound returns and passive income reinforce the income generation cycle
Lifestyle quality influences access to resources that maintain or enhance skill development
Better lifestyle conditions can reinforce investment in education and skill maintenance

summaries.headline: Building personal capital through security and asset returns is the foundational lever for expanding future income capacity.

data.headline: Building personal capital through security and asset returns is the foundational lever for expanding future income capacity.

goal.label: Future Income Capacity
goal.plain: Success looks like a person who has measurably greater ability to generate income in future periods than they do today, because their financial security is solid and their assets are producing returns.
goal.success_signal: A sustained increase in recurring income streams (wages, investment returns, business income) that exceeds the baseline income available at the starting point of the plan.

next_action.label: Define and audit current personal capital assets
next_action.why_now: The graph has a destination but no mapped path or inputs — the immediate unblocked step is to inventory the security and asset-return drivers that the goal depends on, which gates everything else.
next_action.expected_effect: Identifying which assets (skills, savings, investments, relationships) are already present versus missing will reveal the first real bottleneck and allow a prioritized action plan to be built.

reasoning.root_cause: The absence of a defined starting asset base — specifically, the security and asset-return inputs that are described as driving Future Income Capacity but are not yet mapped in the chain.
reasoning.causal_pathway: Financial and human capital (security plus income-generating assets) must first be accumulated or identified. Those assets then produce returns — through employment, investment, or enterprise — and that return flow compounds over time into an enhanced capacity to generate income in future periods. Without the upstream inputs being specified, the goal remains aspirational rather than achievable.
reasoning.effect: If the chain fires correctly, the individual progressively unlocks higher and more resilient income streams, reducing dependence on any single source and increasing optionality over time.
reasoning.why_this_chain_holds: The core assumption is that security and asset returns are both necessary and sufficient inputs to future income capacity — that is, financial stability creates the platform from which assets can be deployed and compounded.

critical_path.label: Personal Security Foundation
critical_path.plain: A stable financial and personal security base (emergency savings, stable housing, health coverage) is the prerequisite that prevents disruption from destroying accumulation progress.

critical_path.label: Asset Accumulation
critical_path.plain: Security frees resources and attention to be directed toward building income-generating assets such as skills, investments, or business equity.

critical_path.label: Asset Returns
critical_path.plain: Accumulated assets begin producing returns — interest, dividends, salary growth, or business profit — which feed back into the system.

critical_path.label: Future Income Capacity
critical_path.plain: Compounding returns and a growing asset base translate directly into an enhanced ability to generate income in future periods, which is the stated goal.

leverage_points.label: Asset Returns feeding back into Asset Accumulation
leverage_points.plain: The reinvestment of returns into further asset-building is the compounding loop at the heart of this chain — small improvements to return rates or reinvestment discipline produce outsized long-term effects on future income capacity.

leverage_points.label: Personal Security Foundation
leverage_points.plain: Security is the enabler of everything downstream; shocks to security (job loss, health crisis, debt crisis) collapse the entire chain, making it the highest-leverage point to protect.

assumptions.plain: Security and asset returns are the primary — and sufficient — drivers of future income capacity; other factors like health, relationships, or macroeconomic conditions are assumed stable or secondary.
assumptions.risk: If macro conditions deteriorate sharply (recession, inflation) or personal factors intervene (illness, family obligations), asset returns may not translate into income capacity growth even when the inputs are present.

assumptions.plain: The individual has sufficient time horizon for compounding to work — this chain implicitly assumes a multi-year or multi-decade window.
assumptions.risk: If the time horizon is short (months rather than years), the chain may not have time to produce measurable improvements in income capacity.

assumptions.plain: Assets held are liquid or deployable enough to generate returns — illiquid or stranded capital does not feed the chain.
assumptions.risk: Capital locked in non-productive or depreciating assets will fail to generate the returns needed to advance toward the goal.

falsifiers: Asset returns are positive and security is maintained, yet future income capacity stagnates or declines — this would indicate a missing mechanism or a broken link between returns and income capacity.
Security is established but assets fail to accumulate, suggesting the ENABLES link between security and asset-building does not hold in practice.
Income capacity improves without security or asset returns being the driver, which would indicate the causal attribution in this chain is wrong and other factors are the real cause.
open_questions: What specific assets are in scope — human capital (skills, education), financial capital (savings, investments), or social capital (network, reputation)? The chain does not specify.
What does 'security' mean operationally — is it an emergency fund threshold, stable employment, insurance coverage, or some combination?
What is the target time horizon over which Future Income Capacity should be measured, and what quantitative improvement counts as success?
Are there known blockers or risks (debt, dependents, career ceiling) that could interrupt the chain and need to be mapped as BLOCKS edges?
Is there a feedback loop — does increased future income capacity reinvest into further security and asset accumulation, or is this a one-time linear chain?

confidence.weakest_link: The entire causal spine between the implicit inputs (security, asset returns) and the goal node is inferred rather than encoded — there are no edges at all in this graph, meaning every causal link is assumed rather than evidenced, making the chain as a whole the weakest element.
